title: Easy 6-Board Leopold Bench
summary: A classic six-board outdoor bench — two 30°-angled leg pairs, a 2x6 backrest, and a 2x10 seat, all cut from three boards with almost no waste.
description:
The Leopold bench is a genuine design classic for a reason: six boards, four of them identical 30°-angled leg cuts, produce a stable, backward-leaning seat with zero fasteners hidden inside joinery — everything is face-screwed and visible, which is exactly what makes this bench buildable in an afternoon by someone who has never built outdoor furniture before.

All the angle cuts are the same 30° off square, and the plan's own tip is worth taking: use the leftover angled offcut from one board as the starting point for the next cut, which conserves wood and keeps every angle consistent without re-measuring each time. A miter saw gives the most accurate, repeatable version of that cut, though a circular saw and speed square get the job done too.

Mark the taller leg pieces using the shorter legs as a physical guide rather than measuring each one independently — tracing directly off an already-cut piece removes an entire source of measurement error. The backrest attaches to the tops of the angled legs first, followed by the seat board, centered on the shorter leg and screwed from the outside of the longer leg — that order, backrest before seat, is what keeps the whole assembly from racking while you're still working with loose parts.

cutList.note: Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point measurement.
Both ends cut at 30° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the boards
steps.body: Cut all boards per the cut list — every angle cut is 30° off square. Conserve wood by using the leftover angled offcut as the starting reference for the next cut. A miter saw is preferred for accuracy and speed. On the taller leg pieces, trim the back off with a circular saw as shown in the reference build, using a speed square to mark the angle.

steps.title: Mark the legs
steps.body: Mark the insides of the taller legs using the shorter legs as a physical guide, tracing directly rather than measuring independently.

steps.title: Join the leg pairs
steps.body: Flip the shorter leg over and match the top lines to the taller leg. Attach with screws so the top line matches the top of the shorter leg.

steps.title: Attach the backrest and seat
steps.body: Attach the 2x6 backrest board first, matching the top edges, with the 2-3/4" screws. Then, on a flat, level surface, attach the seat board centered on the shorter leg and also screw it from the outside of the longer leg.

images.alt: Six-board Leopold bench with angled legs and backrest
